title: Spring Cup Count
desc: The public drinking cups have to be counted, cleaned, and returned before the morning rush.

steps.text:
At the public springs, drinking cups are kept on chained racks so everyone can use them and no one can quietly turn public water into private property. In theory this is a perfect system. In practice, cups go missing, chains kink, and someone always leaves three of them sitting upside down in the wrong basin as if order were a personal insult.

The spring attendant is already taking inventory when you arrive, clay tablet balanced on one forearm. "We should have thirty-two cups on this side," she says. "We have twenty-nine where they belong, one in the wash basin, and two that the morning will reveal to us if it is feeling merciful."

She taps the racks with a counting stick. "Search the side benches and return the missing cups, or scrub and reset the wash basin cups before the early crowds arrive. Either task prevents me from spending breakfast arguing with thirsty strangers."

options.label: Search the benches, ledges, and shade seats for the missing cups
Scrub and reset the cups already in the wash basin

steps.text:
The missing cups are where public things always end up: one on a bench beside a discarded palm pit, one tucked behind a planter where someone clearly meant to return it and clearly did not, and the last hanging from a child's height on the outer rail where it has become part toy and part contraband.

You gather them, check each rim for cracks, and carry them back to the attendant one by one. She marks the tally with visible relief. "Good. Thirty-two. A complete set. Civilization may continue."

Once the cups are rehung, the rack looks simple again - neat chain lengths, clean clay bowls, public order restored before the first real queue forms. The attendant gives the final count a practiced glance and nods. "Excellent. Now if anyone complains about waiting, at least they will be complaining in front of a full rack."

options.label: Hang the last cup and clear the walkway

steps.text:
The wash basin sits off to one side beneath a strip of shade cloth, where the cups are scrubbed with fine sand, rinsed, and checked before they go back on the public rack. This morning's pair are not damaged, just carelessly left. You work the grit around the insides, rinse them clean, and polish the rims with a cloth until the clay goes from dull to warm and smooth under your fingers.

The attendant watches you inspect the chains before rehooking them. One link has twisted hard enough to bind, so you straighten it before the next user discovers the problem with wet hands and no patience. Once the cups are back in place, the rack hangs evenly and the whole spring station regains its precise, orderly look - the kind that makes a line move faster because everyone believes it will.

The attendant scratches the final correction onto her tablet. "There. Now the spring looks governed instead of merely wet." She says it approvingly. At Mipedim Oasis, governance and water are closely related ideas.

options.label: Set down the scrub cloth and let the queue begin
